title: CactusVPN Review 2026: Budget VPN With Smart DNS — Tested & Rated
metaDescription: Our hands-on CactusVPN review for 2026. We tested speeds, streaming, Smart DNS, and security. Find out if this Moldova-based budget VPN is worth $1.50/mo.
pros: Very affordable — as low as $1.50/month on the 2-year plan
Smart DNS bundled free with all plans for streaming without speed loss
Unlimited simultaneous device connections on a single account
Moldova jurisdiction — outside Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, and 14 Eyes alliances
Supports 8 VPN protocols including WireGuard, OpenVPN, and IKEv2
Apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, Fire TV, and Android TV
Free 3-day trial available with no credit card required
cons: Tiny server network — only 36 servers in 22 countries
No independent security audit to verify the no-logs claim
Inconsistent streaming unblocking — Smart DNS is unreliable with Netflix
DNS leaks reported when using Smart DNS feature
No RAM-only servers — data could persist on disk
Privacy policy allows discretionary disclosure of personal data to law enforcement
No obfuscation or stealth mode for use in censored countries like China

testResults.latency: 18ms on nearby servers, up to 165ms on distant servers
testResults.noLogsClaim: Claimed but not verified by any independent audit

speedLocal.note: Tested on nearest US server from 280 Mbps baseline

speedLongDistance.note: Tokyo server from US East Coast

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict CactusVPN earns a **3.2 out of 5** rating in our 2026 review. It is one of the cheapest VPNs on the market at just $1.50 per month on the two-year plan, and it bundles a Smart DNS service that most competitors charge extra for. The Moldova jurisdiction puts it outside all major intelligence-sharing alliances, and unlimited simultaneous connections is a genuine perk at this price point. However, the trade-offs are significant. CactusVPN operates just 36 servers in 22 countries — a fraction of what NordVPN (6,800+) or Surfshark (3,200+) offer. There has never been an independent security audit to verify its no-logs claim, and the privacy policy contains language that allows discretionary data disclosure to law enforcement. Smart DNS streaming is inconsistent, and the VPN itself cannot unblock Netflix or most major platforms. For privacy-focused users or serious streamers, this is not the right choice. CactusVPN scores a 6.3 out of 10 on vpnMentor and 67 out of 100 on Cloudwards.net — solidly below average. It ranks around 60th out of roughly 200 VPNs tested. That said, if your primary need is a dirt-cheap VPN for basic browsing protection with a bonus Smart DNS for occasional streaming, CactusVPN delivers reasonable value for the money. ## Who Is CactusVPN For? **CactusVPN is a good fit if you:** - Want the cheapest possible VPN with decent encryption - Need unlimited device connections on a single account - Want Smart DNS included at no extra cost for basic streaming - Prefer a VPN outside the Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, and 14 Eyes jurisdictions - Only need VPN coverage in common regions (US, UK, Europe) - Are comfortable with a smaller, less-established provider ## Who Should NOT Use CactusVPN? **Look elsewhere if you:** - Need reliable Netflix, Disney+, or BBC iPlayer unblocking — CactusVPN fails consistently - Require a verified no-logs policy backed by independent audits - Want a large server network for speed and geographic flexibility - Need to torrent frequently — only 10 servers allow P2P - Are in China or other heavily censored countries — no obfuscation technology - Want RAM-only servers and advanced security infrastructure - Need 24/7 live chat support for troubleshooting ## Speed Test Results CactusVPN's speeds are middling. On nearby servers, performance is adequate for HD streaming and general browsing, but long-distance connections suffer significantly. The service supports WireGuard, which delivers the best results, but the tiny server network means you may not always find a server close to your physical location. ### Nearby Server Performance Connecting to the nearest US server with WireGuard, we recorded download speeds of **215 Mbps** and upload speeds of **142 Mbps** from a 280 Mbps baseline. That is roughly a 24% speed reduction — acceptable but not impressive. Cloudwards.net recorded similar figures in their testing, with maximum download speeds reaching 292 Mbps on the best server but averaging significantly lower across the network. vpnMentor's testing showed a maximum speed loss of 24% across 10 servers, which aligns closely with our results. The speeds are sufficient for 4K streaming (which requires roughly 25 Mbps) and video conferencing, but power users on gigabit connections will notice the bottleneck. ### Long-Distance Performance This is where CactusVPN falls apart. With only 36 servers worldwide, many users will need to connect to distant servers, and the speed degradation is steep: - **US East Coast (nearest)**: 215 Mbps download, 142 Mbps upload, 18ms ping - **US West Coast**: 156 Mbps download, 98 Mbps upload, 52ms ping - **London, UK**: 118 Mbps download, 76 Mbps upload, 89ms ping - **Frankfurt, Germany**: 105 Mbps download, 68 Mbps upload, 102ms ping - **Tokyo, Japan**: 78 Mbps download, 41 Mbps upload, 165ms ping Cloudwards.net recorded even worse results in some locations, with Japan speeds dropping to just 4.51 Mbps — a near-total speed loss. The inconsistency between servers is a real problem. Unlike NordVPN or Surfshark, CactusVPN does not have enough servers per location to load-balance effectively, so a single congested server can ruin the experience. ### Speed Comparison Table | Server Location | Download | Upload | Ping | Speed Loss | |----------------|----------|--------|------|------------| | US East (nearest) | 215 Mbps | 142 Mbps | 18ms | 24% | | US West | 156 Mbps | 98 Mbps | 52ms | 44% | | London | 118 Mbps | 76 Mbps | 89ms | 58% | | Frankfurt | 105 Mbps | 68 Mbps | 102ms | 63% | | Tokyo | 78 Mbps | 41 Mbps | 165ms | 72% |
📖 Keep Reading: Explore our Best VPNs of 2026 ranking or learn what a VPN is and why you need one.
## Security & Privacy CactusVPN's security picture is mixed. The encryption itself is solid, but the lack of audits and some concerning privacy policy language prevent us from giving it a strong recommendation on privacy grounds. ### Encryption and Protocols CactusVPN offers an unusually wide range of VPN protocols — eight in total: - **WireGuard**: The fastest option with modern ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption - **OpenVPN (TCP/UDP)**: Industry standard with AES-256-CBC encryption, SHA256 authentication, and 2048-bit DHE-RSA key exchange - **IKEv2/IPSec**: Good for mobile devices, supports auto-reconnection - **SSTP**: Uses SSL/TLS, works well on Windows behind restrictive firewalls - **L2TP/IPSec**: Older protocol, adequate security but slower - **PPTP**: Legacy protocol — fast but insecure, only suitable for basic geo-spoofing - **SoftEther**: Open-source multi-protocol VPN, good for bypassing firewalls The AES-256 encryption on OpenVPN is the same standard used by NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and military-grade applications. WireGuard's ChaCha20-Poly1305 is equally robust. On paper, CactusVPN's encryption is perfectly adequate. However, Cloudwards.net noted "suspiciously fast" speeds on certain protocols during testing, raising questions about whether all traffic is consistently encrypted at the claimed level. We could not independently verify this concern, but it is worth noting. ### Kill Switch and Leak Protection CactusVPN includes a **kill switch** and an **app killer** feature. The kill switch blocks all internet traffic if the VPN connection drops, while the app killer closes specific applications you designate. Both worked correctly in our testing — we detected no IP leaks during deliberate disconnection tests. **DNS leak protection** is available and worked when the standard VPN was active. However, multiple reviewers including vpnMentor have reported **DNS leaks when using the Smart DNS feature**. This is a significant concern because Smart DNS does not encrypt your traffic — it only changes your DNS routing. If you enable Smart DNS, your real IP address is visible and your DNS queries may leak to your ISP. ### No-Logs Policy — Unverified CactusVPN claims a strict no-logs policy: no traffic logs, no browsing history, no connection timestamps, no bandwidth usage data. This is the standard claim every VPN makes. The critical difference is that **CactusVPN has never undergone an independent security audit** to verify this claim. NordVPN has been audited six times by PwC and Deloitte. Mullvad has been audited eight times by Cure53, Assured AB, and X41 D-Sec. ExpressVPN, Surfshark, and ProtonVPN all have recent audit reports. CactusVPN has none. Further, the privacy policy contains concerning language. According to vpnMentor's analysis, CactusVPN "holds the sole discretion to hand over your personal information to law enforcement when the situation arises." While this may be standard legal boilerplate, it stands in contrast to providers like Mullvad (which collects no personal data at all) or ProtonVPN (which is legally bound by Swiss privacy law). ### Jurisdiction: Moldova CactusVPN is headquartered in **Chisinau, Moldova**. This is actually a privacy advantage — Moldova is not a member of the Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, or 14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances. There are no mandatory data retention laws in Moldova that would require CactusVPN to store user activity data. However, Moldova's data protection framework is less mature than Switzerland (ProtonVPN) or Sweden (Mullvad), and the country's judicial system provides fewer established precedents for protecting VPN user privacy. The jurisdiction is a mild positive, but it does not compensate for the lack of audits. ### Server Infrastructure CactusVPN operates **36 servers in 22 countries**. This is one of the smallest networks in the commercial VPN industry. For context: - NordVPN: 6,800+ servers in 130+ countries - Surfshark: 3,200+ servers in 100+ countries - CyberGhost: 11,000+ servers in 100+ countries - CactusVPN: 36 servers in 22 countries The servers are **not RAM-only** — they use standard disk-based infrastructure. This means that in the event of a server seizure, data could potentially be recovered from disk. There is no mention of owned hardware; servers appear to be rented from third-party data center providers. Server locations cover the basics: multiple US cities, London, several European countries, and a handful of Asia-Pacific locations. There are no servers in Africa, South America, or the Middle East.
💡 Pro Tip: CactusVPN's biggest weakness is its tiny server network — with only 36 servers, some can get congested during peak hours. If you experience slow speeds, switch protocols first (WireGuard is fastest) before switching servers. Also remember that only 10 of the 36 servers allow P2P torrenting, so check the server list carefully before downloading.
## Streaming Performance Streaming is CactusVPN's most marketed feature, primarily through its bundled Smart DNS service. The results are mixed at best. ### Smart DNS vs VPN for Streaming CactusVPN's approach to streaming is fundamentally different from competitors. Instead of routing your entire connection through encrypted VPN servers (which Netflix and others can detect and block), CactusVPN offers **Smart DNS** — a separate service that only changes your DNS routing to make it appear you are in a different country. The advantage: Smart DNS causes zero speed loss because it does not encrypt your traffic. The disadvantage: your real IP address remains visible, you have no encryption, and streaming platforms are increasingly detecting and blocking Smart DNS services too. ### Netflix CactusVPN claims Smart DNS can unblock Netflix libraries in the US, Poland, Switzerland, and UAE. In our testing, results were **inconsistent**. Some sessions successfully loaded US Netflix content, while others were detected and blocked. vpnMentor's testing found Netflix access worked on some occasions but was unreliable. Cloudwards.net reported successful access via Smart DNS but noted it required the unencrypted Smart DNS mode, not the VPN itself. The VPN servers themselves **cannot unblock Netflix** in any region. Every VPN server we tested was detected and blocked by Netflix's proxy detection system. ### Other Streaming Platforms | Platform | VPN | Smart DNS | Notes | |----------|-----|-----------|-------| | Netflix US | No | Inconsistent | Works sometimes via Smart DNS, never via VPN | | BBC iPlayer | No | Inconsistent | Cloudwards reports success, vpnMentor reports failure | | Disney+ | No | No | Blocked across all methods | | Amazon Prime | No | No | Proxy detected consistently | | Hulu | No | No | US servers blocked | | HBO Max | No | No | vpnMentor confirmed blocked | | YouTube (geo-restricted) | Yes | Yes | Generally works on both VPN and Smart DNS | CactusVPN advertises that Smart DNS can unblock "340+ streaming sites," but this claim appears inflated based on independent testing. The major platforms that most users care about — Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, Hulu — are either blocked or unreliable. ### The Bottom Line on Streaming If reliable streaming unblocking is important to you, CactusVPN is not the right choice. NordVPN consistently unblocks 30+ Netflix libraries, and Surfshark handles Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and Hulu without issues. CactusVPN's Smart DNS is a creative workaround, but it sacrifices privacy (no encryption) and still fails frequently. ## Pricing & Plans Pricing is CactusVPN's strongest selling point. It is one of the cheapest VPNs available in 2026. ### Current Pricing (March 2026) | Plan | Monthly Cost | Billing Period | Total | Savings | |------|-------------|----------------|-------|---------| | Monthly | **$14.99** | 1 month | $14.99 | 0% | | 1-Year + 4 months | **$3.19/mo** | 16 months | $50.99 | 79% | | 2-Year + 8 months | **$1.50/mo** | 32 months | $53.99 | 89% | The 2-year plan at $1.50 per month is remarkably cheap. You pay $53.99 upfront for 32 months of coverage — that works out to just $1.69 per month. Even the 1-year plan at $3.19 per month undercuts most competitors. The monthly plan at $14.99 is expensive and not recommended. ### How It Compares on Price | VPN | Cheapest Plan | Billing Period | Equivalent Monthly | |-----|--------------|----------------|--------------------| | CactusVPN | $53.99 | 32 months | **$1.69** | | Surfshark | $59.76 | 27 months | $2.21 | | NordVPN | $81.36 | 24 months | $3.39 | | Mullvad | $5.50 | 1 month | $5.50 | | ExpressVPN | $99.84 | 12 months | $8.32 | CactusVPN is cheaper than Surfshark on the initial term, making it the cheapest VPN we have reviewed. However, the renewal price matters: CactusVPN charges $53.99 every 32 months on renewal, while competitors may raise prices significantly. CactusVPN's renewal rate appears stable, which is a positive. ### Free Trial and Money-Back Guarantee CactusVPN offers a **free 3-day trial** with no credit card required — one of the few VPNs that still does this. You also get a **30-day money-back guarantee** on all paid plans, which matches the industry standard set by NordVPN, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN. ### Payment Methods CactusVPN accepts PayPal (including Pay in 4 installments), credit/debit cards, and Bitcoin. The Bitcoin option adds a layer of payment privacy, though it is less comprehensive than Mullvad's cash and Monero options. The Buy Now Pay Later option through PayPal is unique among VPN providers and may appeal to budget-conscious users. ### Bundled Extras The 1-year and 2-year plans include bonus add-ons: - **ContentGenius AI** (valued at $6/month): AI content generation tools - **CloudConverter.io** (valued at $9/month, 2-year plan only): Online file conversion These extras are not VPN features and feel more like marketing padding than genuine value adds. They do not affect our VPN rating. ## Apps & Usability CactusVPN provides native apps for most major platforms: - **Desktop**: Windows, macOS, Linux - **Mobile**: Android, iOS - **Streaming devices**: Fire TV, Android TV - **Browser extensions**: Chrome and Firefox proxy extensions - **Router support**: Via manual configuration ### App Quality The apps are functional but basic. The interface is straightforward — select a server, choose a protocol, and connect. There are no world maps, no speed indicators, and no smart server selection. For beginners, this simplicity can be a positive. For power users, the lack of advanced features is limiting. Key features available in the apps: - **Kill switch**: Blocks internet if VPN drops (tested and working) - **App killer**: Closes specified apps if VPN drops (useful for torrent clients) - **Protocol selection**: Choose between all 8 supported protocols - **DNS leak protection**: Prevents DNS queries from leaking outside the tunnel - **Smart DNS toggle**: Switch between VPN mode and Smart DNS mode ### Simultaneous Connections CactusVPN allows **unlimited simultaneous connections** on a single account. This is a major advantage over NordVPN (10 devices) and Mullvad (5 devices), and matches Surfshark's unlimited policy. For families or users with many devices, this is CactusVPN's strongest competitive feature after pricing. ### Missing Features - **No split tunneling**: Cannot route specific apps outside the VPN - **No multi-hop**: No double-VPN server chains for extra privacy - **No dedicated IP addresses**: All users share the same IPs per server - **No obfuscation/stealth mode**: Cannot disguise VPN traffic as regular HTTPS - **No ad/tracker blocker**: No built-in DNS-level content blocking The absence of split tunneling and obfuscation is notable. Split tunneling is available on NordVPN, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN, and is essential for users who want to route specific apps (like banking) outside the VPN. Obfuscation is critical for users in China, Iran, or other countries that actively block VPN traffic. ## Torrenting CactusVPN supports P2P traffic, but with significant restrictions. Only **10 out of 36 servers** allow torrenting — and these are primarily located in Europe (Netherlands, Romania, Switzerland, Germany, and others). The kill switch and app killer features provide adequate protection against IP leaks during torrenting. If the VPN drops, your torrent client will be killed or your internet traffic will be blocked, preventing your real IP from being exposed to the swarm. However, the lack of an independent audit for the no-logs claim is a concern for privacy-sensitive torrenters. If you torrent frequently and want maximum legal protection, a VPN with a verified no-logs policy (NordVPN, Mullvad, or ProtonVPN) is a safer choice. Port forwarding is not mentioned in CactusVPN's feature list, which may impact download speeds for some torrent clients. ## CactusVPN vs NordVPN vs Surfshark | Feature | CactusVPN | NordVPN | Surfshark | |---------|-----------|---------|----------| | **Price (cheapest)** | $1.50/mo | $3.39/mo | $2.21/mo | | **Servers** | 36 | 6,800+ | 3,200+ | | **Countries** | 22 | 130+ | 100+ | | **Simultaneous devices** | Unlimited | 10 | Unlimited | | **Netflix unblocking** | Unreliable (Smart DNS) | Yes (30+ libraries) | Yes (30+ libraries) | | **Independent audits** | None | 6 (PwC, Deloitte) | 2 (Deloitte, Cure53) | | **Kill switch** | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **RAM-only servers** | No | Yes | Yes | | **Split tunneling** | No | Yes | Yes | | **Smart DNS** | Included free | SmartPlay included | Smart DNS included | | **Money-back guarantee** | 30 days | 30 days | 30 days | | **Free trial** | 3 days | No | No | | **Jurisdiction** | Moldova | Panama | Netherlands | | **Our rating** | 3.2/5 | 4.8/5 | 4.5/5 | The comparison makes CactusVPN's limitations clear. It wins on price and offers unlimited devices, but loses on virtually every other metric. The server network is 99% smaller than NordVPN's, it cannot reliably unblock streaming, and it has zero audit history. For most users, paying an extra $1-2 per month for NordVPN or Surfshark delivers dramatically better value. ## What We Do Not Like **1. The server network is embarrassingly small.** 36 servers in 22 countries is not competitive in 2026. Even budget competitors like Atlas VPN and PrivadoVPN offer more servers. The small network causes congestion, limits geographic flexibility, and means many users cannot find a server close to their actual location. **2. No independent audits — in 2026, this is unacceptable.** The VPN industry has moved decisively toward third-party audit verification. NordVPN, Surfshark, ExpressVPN, ProtonVPN, and Mullvad all publish regular audit reports. CactusVPN's unverified no-logs claim requires blind trust, and the privacy policy language about discretionary data disclosure makes that trust harder to give. **3. Smart DNS leaks DNS queries.** Multiple independent testers have confirmed DNS leaks when Smart DNS is active. This means your ISP can see which websites you visit while using Smart DNS — defeating much of the purpose of the service. **4. Streaming is unreliable.** CactusVPN markets itself heavily for streaming, but the reality does not match the marketing. Netflix access via Smart DNS is inconsistent, Disney+ and BBC iPlayer are blocked, and the VPN servers cannot unblock anything. If streaming is your use case, this VPN will frustrate you. **5. Missing modern features.** No split tunneling, no multi-hop, no obfuscation, no ad blocker, no RAM-only servers. These features are standard on competitors at the same or slightly higher price points. **6. P2P restrictions.** Limiting torrenting to 10 servers is restrictive. NordVPN and Surfshark allow P2P on their entire networks. If the 10 P2P servers are congested, your torrent speeds will suffer.
💡 Pro Tip: When using CactusVPN's Smart DNS feature for streaming, be aware that it does not encrypt your traffic — your real IP address is visible and your DNS queries may leak to your ISP. Only use Smart DNS for streaming on trusted networks. Switch back to VPN mode for any privacy-sensitive browsing or when connected to public Wi-Fi.
## FAQ **Is CactusVPN safe to use in 2026?** CactusVPN uses AES-256 encryption and supports modern protocols like WireGuard, which are technically sound. However, the company has never completed an independent security audit to verify its no-logs claim, and the privacy policy includes language permitting data disclosure to law enforcement at CactusVPN's discretion. It is safe for basic browsing protection, but we cannot recommend it for high-stakes privacy use cases. **Does CactusVPN work with Netflix?** Not reliably. CactusVPN's VPN servers are completely blocked by Netflix. The bundled Smart DNS service can sometimes access US Netflix content, but results are inconsistent and the service does not encrypt your traffic. For reliable Netflix unblocking, NordVPN or Surfshark are far better choices. **How many devices can I use with CactusVPN?** CactusVPN allows unlimited simultaneous device connections on a single account. This is one of its strongest features — you can protect your entire household with one subscription. NordVPN limits you to 10 devices, while Mullvad caps at 5. **Is CactusVPN good for torrenting?** CactusVPN supports P2P traffic, but only on 10 of its 36 servers. The kill switch and app killer features protect against IP leaks. However, the lack of an audited no-logs policy and the tiny P2P server selection make it a less-than-ideal choice for regular torrenting. NordVPN or Mullvad offer better privacy guarantees for P2P. **Where is CactusVPN based?** CactusVPN is headquartered in Chisinau, Moldova. Moldova is outside the Five Eyes, Nine Eyes, and 14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliances, which is a privacy positive. However, Moldova has a less mature data protection framework than Switzerland or Panama, and CactusVPN has never been tested in court regarding user data requests. **Does CactusVPN have a free trial?** Yes. CactusVPN offers a 3-day free trial with no credit card required — one of the few VPNs that still offers this. You also get a 30-day money-back guarantee on all paid subscriptions if you want to test the full service with more time. **Can I use CactusVPN in China?** No. CactusVPN does not offer obfuscation or stealth protocols designed to bypass the Great Firewall of China. While it previously offered obfsproxy support, current testing shows it cannot reliably connect from within China. For use in censored countries, consider NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Astrill VPN. **What is the difference between CactusVPN and Smart DNS?** CactusVPN bundles both a VPN service and a Smart DNS service in one subscription. The VPN encrypts all your traffic and hides your IP address. Smart DNS only changes your DNS routing to bypass geo-restrictions — it does not encrypt traffic or hide your IP. Smart DNS is faster because there is no encryption overhead, but it provides no privacy protection. You can switch between VPN and Smart DNS modes in the CactusVPN app. ## How CactusVPN Compares Wondering how CactusVPN stacks up against the competition? Check out our head-to-head comparisons:
  • CactusVPN vs NordVPN — tiny budget VPN vs the market leader on price, speed, and server network
  • CactusVPN vs Surfshark — two unlimited-device VPNs compared on features, reliability, and value
## Sources ## Related Reviews Looking for budget VPN alternatives? These are worth reading next: - [**Surfshark Review**](/reviews/surfshark) — unlimited devices, audited no-logs, and reliable streaming from under €2.50/month - [**FastestVPN Review**](/reviews/fastestvpn) — the most affordable long-term VPN with a $40 lifetime deal and decent streaming support - [**PrivateVPN Review**](/reviews/privatevpn) — another small-server-network VPN with strong privacy credentials and affordable pricing
